title: 2 Peter
author: Peter, the apostle (some scholars dispute; 2 Pet. 3:1 presupposes 1 Peter by the same author)
setting: Peter writes from awareness of his approaching death (1:14) to counter the growing influence of false teachers who deny the return of Christ and use Christian freedom as license for immorality.
purpose: To call the church to grow in knowledge of Christ and the Scriptures, to warn against false teachers who distort the apostolic message, and to affirm the certain coming of the Day of the Lord despite the scoffers.
themes: Growth in Christian virtue and knowledge
The divine origin and authority of Scripture (1:20-21)
Warning against false teachers and their destruction
The Day of the Lord — certain, though delayed
God's patience, not weakness: 'not wishing that any should perish'
The new heavens and new earth

outline.label: The Day of the Lord and the New Creation

key_people.name: Peter the Apostle
key_people.role: Writing as an eyewitness of the Transfiguration (1:16-18) who is aware his death is near (1:14); his appeal to personal experience of Christ's majesty gives the letter's arguments their pastoral weight

key_people.name: The False Teachers
key_people.role: The unnamed opponents who are characterised in the sharpest terms in 2:1-22 — promising freedom while being slaves to corruption; their coming was predicted by the prophets and apostles

context: 2 Peter was written c. AD 65-67, Peter's last letter before his martyrdom. It addresses false teachers who are denying the Lord's return and using this denial to justify moral license. The letter borrows extensively from Jude and is the NT's most direct polemic against a proto-Gnostic threat to apostolic teaching.
christ_connection: 2 Peter's christological emphasis falls on the glory and majesty of Christ at the Transfiguration (1:16-18 — Peter is the only NT author who identifies himself as an eyewitness of this event) and on his certain return. The Transfiguration is adduced as eyewitness evidence for 'the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ' (1:16) — it is a preview of the parousia. The prophetic word (the OT Scriptures, 1:19-21) provides written corroboration of what Peter and the apostles saw. Against those who scoff at the delay of the return, Peter argues that God's timing is not human timing, and that the delay is the expression of his patience toward the repentance of all (3:9).

key_verses.note: 'He has granted to us his precious and very great promises, so that through them you may become partakers of the divine nature' — the most explicit NT statement of theosis (participation in God's nature through the promises)
'No prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation... men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit' — the organic, Spirit-borne account of Scripture's inspiration

'The Lord is not slow to fulfil his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance' — the theological explanation for the delay of the parousia

themes_detail.title: The Apostolic Eyewitness and the Trustworthy Prophetic Word
themes_detail.text: 2 Peter 1:16-21 establishes a two-tier argument for Christian confidence: the eyewitness testimony of the apostles (specifically the Transfiguration, which Peter himself saw, heard, and reports) and the prophetic Scriptures, which are even more certain than eyewitness experience because they come from God through the Spirit. The argument's structure is significant: apostolic eyewitness testimony and scriptural prophecy are not in competition but in coordination — both point to the same coming Lord. Against teachers who deny the parousia, Peter adduces personal experience and prophetic testimony as mutually reinforcing evidence.

themes_detail.title: The False Teachers and Their Destruction
themes_detail.text: Chapter 2 is the NT's most sustained and graphic polemic against false teachers — more detailed even than Jude (which it partially quotes). The false teachers promise freedom but are themselves slaves to corruption (2:19); they have knowledge of Christ but have returned to their former defilement (2:20-22, the dog returning to its vomit). The theological danger is not merely moral licence but the denial of the Lord who bought them (2:1) — a repudiation of the atonement. Peter's three OT examples (fallen angels, Noah's flood, Sodom and Gomorrah) establish that God's judgment on ungodliness is certain, historical, and pattern-setting.

themes_detail.title: The Day of the Lord and the New Creation
themes_detail.text: Chapter 3 addresses the scoffers who argue that the promise of the Lord's coming has been shown to be false by the delay. Peter's three responses are: God's timing is not human timing (1000 years as a day, 3:8); the delay is patient mercy, not failure (3:9); and when it comes, it will be sudden and total — heavens and earth dissolved (3:10-12). The goal is a new heavens and new earth in which righteousness dwells (3:13), quoting Isaiah 65-66. This eschatological horizon is the motivation for present holy living: 'what sort of people ought you to be in lives of holiness and godliness, waiting for and hastening the coming of the day of God' (3:11-12).

themes_detail.title: Growth in Grace and Knowledge as the Letter's Practical Goal
themes_detail.text: Against the false teachers who claim superior knowledge (gnosis), Peter calls for growth in the genuine knowledge of Jesus Christ (1:2, 3, 8; 3:18) combined with a progression of virtues: faith, virtue, knowledge, self-control, steadfastness, godliness, brotherly affection, love (1:5-7). This 'divine nature' participation (1:4) is not philosophical speculation but ethical transformation — the false teachers, despite their claims, are characterised by moral corruption. True knowledge of Christ produces godliness; a claim to spiritual knowledge that produces licentiousness is self-refuting. The letter ends where it began: grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.
